source_metadata.title_raw: Robert Schulz - A-19-054 - May 8, 2019 - San Diego

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Robert Schulz 
Associate Vice President 
San Diego State University 
5500 Campanile Drive 
San Diego, CA 92182-1620 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-054 
Dear Mr. Schulz: 
May 8, 2019 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
Government Code section 1090. Please note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice 
(In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete 
and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you 
should contact us for additional advice. 
In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all 
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the San Diego County 
District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.l(c)(3).) We did not receive a 
written response from either entity. (Section 1097. l(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, 
for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding 
against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.l(c)(5).) 
QUESTION 
Does Section 1090 prohibit the general contractor for San Diego State University (the 
"University") from entering a subcontract with an architectural firm to provide architectural design 
services for a project where the architectural firm performed a feasibility study under a previous 
contract for the same project? 
CONCLUSION 
No. Section 1090 does not prohibit the University's general contractor from subcontracting 
with the architectural firm to provide architectural design services for the project. 


FACTS 
FileNo. A-19-054 
Page No. 2 
You are the Associate Vice President of San Diego State University ("University") seeking 
advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of Section 1090 on behalf of the University. 
The University has for years been exploring the viability of building a new multi-use 
stadium as part of a larger redevelopment of the SDCCU Stadium site in Mission Valley 
("Project"). In August 2017, the University issued a Request for Qualifications (RFQ) and Request 
for Interview for services related to a feasibility study to evaluate different stadium alternatives on 
the SDCCU Stadium site and on campus. 
During the competitive process, all architectural firms interviewed for the assignment were 
told that the engagement was limited to only the feasibility study and that there were absolutely no 
assurances for any future role on the project. On October 3, 2017, the University executed a 
contract with Populous to prepare the Stadium Feasibility Design Study ("Feasibility Study"). 
Under the Feasibility Study contract, Populous was required to provide: (1) Information 
Gathering and Document Review; (2) Kick-Off M~eting, Visioning Session and Site Visit; (3) 
Programming Confirmation Meeting; (4) Program Definition and Finalization; (5) Conceptual 
Design - Conceptual Site Plans, Building Plans and Sketches; (6) Cost Estimating - Conceptual 
Estimating and ROM Pricing; (7) Presentation of Draft Report; and (8) Final Report. 
Populous performed master planning, programmatic development and conceptual design 
work at the direction of the University. The direction provided by the University was informed by 
years of internal analysis, a comprehensive market study and associated revenue models. All the 
studies and work product related to the final Feasibility Study, including market analysis and 
conceptual designs, are the property of the University and have been used to inform key University 
decision makers. 
Although the Feasibility Study provided critical information including the optimal site for 
the stadium, associated conceptual designs, and potential costs and revenues it has always been the 
intention of the University to start from scratch once the General Contractor leading the Design 
Build Team was brought on board. 
In January 2018, the Feasibility Study was released to the public. In February 2019 Clark 
Construction Company was selected as the General Contractor to lead the stadium project. In March 
2019, Clark issued the RFP for Architectural Design Services related to the Design and 
Preconstruction of the new Aztec Stadium project for which Populous previously provided a 
conceptual design study. This RFP includes an addendum with the Feasibility Study created by 
Populous. Clark, in conjunction with the University, has recently commenced the competitive 
selection process and Populous was among the short listed architectural firms that were notified on 
February 27, 2019. 


ANALYSIS 
File No. A-19-054 
Page No. 3 
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with 
financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from 
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their 
agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the 
making of a contract in which the official has a financial interest." (People v. Honig (1996) 48 
Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 
Cal.3d 633, 646.) 
Section 1090 provides, in part, that "[m]embers of the Legislature, state, county, district, 
judicial district, and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract 
made by them in their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are members." 
Independent Contractors Subject to Section I 090 
Courts have long found that independent contractors that serve in advisory positions that 
have a potential to exert considerable influence over the contracting decisions of a public agency 
are subject to Section 1090. (See Hub City Solid Waste Services, Inc. v. City of Compton (2010) 186 
Cal.App.4th 1114, 1124-1125; Schaefer v. Berinstein (1956) 140 Cal.App.2d 278,291 ["statutes 
prohibiting personal interests of public officers in public contracts are strictly enforced. [Citation.] 
... [if] A person merely in an advisory position to a city is affected by the conflicts of interest rule"].) 
This long-standing rule was recently affirmed by the California Supreme Court (People v. Superior 
Court (Sahlolbei) (2017) 3 Cal.5th 230), and it applies equally to corporate consultants. (Davis v. 
Fresno Unified School District (2015) 23 7 Cal.App.4th 261, 300.) 
The purpose behind this inclusiveness of the definition is to ensure that independent 
contractors who are essentially performing a public function, though temporarily, provide the same 
"fealty expected from permanent officers and employees." (46 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen 74 (1965).) 
In Hub City, the court stated that a person's status as an official under Section 1090 "turns on the 
extent to which the person influences an agency's contracting decisions or otherwise acts in a 
capacity that demands the public trust." (Hub City, supra, at p. 1125.) 
Most recently, the California Supreme Court confirmed that independent contractors are 
subject to Section 1090. In Sahlolbei, the Supreme Court concluded that Section 1090' s reference to 
"officers" applies to "outside advisors with responsibilities for public contracting similar to those 
belonging to formal officers" (Id. at 23 7), and held that not all independent contractors are covered 
by Section 1090 - liability extends only to independent contractors who can be said to have been 
entrusted with "transact[ing] on behalf of the Government" (Id. at 240 quoting Stigall, supra, 58 
Cal.2d at 570.) 
Here, the determinative question is whether under the initial Feasibility Study contract, 
Populous had responsibilities for public contracting similar to those of University employees such 
that Populous is covered by Section 1090 in the first instance. We conclude that it did not. 


File No. A-19-054 
Page No. 4 
In Hub City, supra, the City of Compton sought to bring its waste services in-house in order 
to save money after years of granting multi-year franchises to private vendors. (Id. at 1119.) The 
City sought advice from Michael Aloyan, a waste management expert it had consulted in the past, 
and entered into a management agreement with Aloyan's one-man company, American Utilities 
Services ("AUS"). (Ibid.) 
Under the agreement, AUS was an independent contractor but Aloyan's duties included 
supervising City staff, negotiating City contracts, and purchasing equipment and real estate on 
behalf of the City. (Hub City, supra, at 1120.) Knowing the City needed money and prior to the 
expiration of the agreement, he formed a new company, HUB, and approached City officials with 
an offer to take over waste collection as a franchisee. (Ibid.) The City Council approved his offer of 
$2 million up front and $700,000 per year for a 15-year franchise that allegedly may have been 
worth up to $100 million. The court ultimately found that Aloyan was a public official for purposes 
of Section 10901 who made a contract in his official capacity in which he had a prohibitory 
financial interest. 
Here, unlike the situation in Hub City, Populous did not have public contracting duties under 
the Feasibility Study contract that brought it within the ambit of Section 1090. For example, 
Feasibility Study contract did not require Populous to supervise University staff, negotiate contracts 
or make purchases on the University's behalf. Instead, Populous, which had no decision-making 
authority, was required to perform conceptual design work for a new multi-use stadium at the 
direction of the University, which had been informed by years of internal analysis and studies. 
Additionally, all work produced by Populous under the Feasibility Study contract is the property of 
the University and has been used to inform key University decision makers. Therefore, based on 
these facts, the Feasibility Study contract did not entrust Populous with duties to engage in or advise 
on contracting on behalf of the University; and it is thus not prohibited under Section 1090 from 
bidding or entering into a subsequent contract. 
Accordingly, Section 1090 does not prohibit the University's General Contractor from sub-
contracting with Populous to provide Architectural Design Services related to the Project. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
1 The court initially found that HUB was Aloyan's alter ego for purposes of Section 1090.

sections.question:
Does Section 1090 prohibit the general contractor for San Diego State University (the 
"University") from entering a subcontract with an architectural firm to provide architectural design 
services for a project where the architectural firm performed a feasibility study under a previous 
contract for the same project?
sections.conclusion:
No. Section 1090 does not prohibit the University's general contractor from subcontracting 
with the architectural firm to provide architectural design services for the project.

"University") from entering a subcontract with an architectural firm to provide architectural design 
services for a project where the architectural firm performed a feasibility study under a previous 
contract for the same project?

CONCLUSION: No. Section 1090 does not prohibit the University's general contractor from subcontracting 
with the architectural firm to provide architectural design services for the project.
